title: Solidity contracts on DuskEVM
summary: Deploy Ethereum-style smart contracts with Solidity, Foundry or Hardhat, EVM wallets, DUSK gas, and Blockscout inspection.
beginnerUse: Choose this when your project would normally be an Ethereum dApp and privacy is not the first thing you need to prove locally.
safeNextStep: Start with the DuskEVM Setup step, run the RPC health check, then create the Foundry starter.

title: EVM wallet, RPC, and explorer tooling
summary: Use EIP-1193 wallets, DuskEVM Testnet chain metadata, RPC health checks, and Blockscout links to keep app, wallet, and explorer aligned.
beginnerUse: Choose this when the main risk is sending a transaction on the wrong chain or debugging an unreachable RPC endpoint.
safeNextStep: Confirm chain ID 745 / 0x2e9 before funding or deploying.

title: DuskEVM testnet funding bridge
summary: Fund an EVM testnet wallet with practice DUSK through the official DuskDS to DuskEVM bridge route.
beginnerUse: Choose this after your wallet is on the right chain and before you try to deploy a contract.
safeNextStep: Follow the official bridge guide and avoid duplicate bridge attempts while a transfer is pending.

title: Confidential EVM flows through Hedger
summary: Dusk docs describe Hedger as the route toward confidential flows for EVM applications, but the audited public docs/repos do not yet provide enough detail for a guided Studio deploy path.
beginnerUse: Treat this as a product direction to track if your Solidity app needs privacy later, not as the first local build path.
safeNextStep: Build the public DuskEVM loop first, then wait for source-backed Hedger docs before automating templates or deploys.

title: Native Rust/WASM smart contracts
summary: Build native Dusk contracts with Rust, WASM, DuskVM, Dusk Forge, VM-backed tests, and paired contract plus data-driver artifacts.
beginnerUse: Choose this when your app needs Dusk-native execution, protocol-level control, or privacy-aware flows close to the base layer.
safeNextStep: Run DuskDS preflight, create the Forge starter, then build contract WASM and data-driver WASM together.

title: Data drivers, schemas, calls, and events
summary: Use data-driver WASM and RUES driver endpoints to encode inputs, decode outputs, inspect schemas, and keep frontends aligned with native contracts.
beginnerUse: Choose this after your first Forge build works and before you connect a frontend or indexer to a contract.
safeNextStep: Keep contract WASM and data-driver WASM from the same source commit; rebuild both after contract changes.

title: Dusk Connect wallet integration
summary: Discover compatible Dusk wallets, request profiles or shielded addresses, switch networks, send transactions, sign messages, and connect app code through a small SDK.
beginnerUse: Choose this when your dApp needs users to connect a Dusk wallet instead of pasting addresses or secrets into your UI.
safeNextStep: Use Dusk Connect for the wallet side and W3sper or node APIs for network reads and transaction lifecycle handling.

title: Dusk Wallet Extension provider
summary: Use the first-party browser wallet as a compatible provider for public and shielded account flows across mainnet, testnet, devnet, or custom nodes.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you need to test how a real browser wallet appears to a Dusk dApp.
safeNextStep: Start from Dusk Connect, then use the wallet extension docs when testing provider discovery and account requests.

title: W3sper app and node SDK
summary: Connect JavaScript apps and scripts to Dusk nodes, create profiles, read Moonlight balances, submit transfers, and query GraphQL without hand-rolling raw RUES calls.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you want to read network state or submit native transactions from app code.
safeNextStep: Run the W3sper Testnet connection snippet before using raw HTTP endpoints.

title: Rusk Wallet manual operations
summary: Use the official Rusk Wallet CLI for manual profile, balance, transfer, staking, contract-call, contract-deploy, contract-id, blob, and custom-node workflows when browser wallet flows are not enough.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you need a terminal wallet for native DuskDS operations and you are ready to handle signing, funding, node URLs, fees, and finality deliberately.
safeNextStep: Read the Rusk Wallet docs, keep secrets out of Studio/browser forms, and run manual testnet commands before considering any deploy automation.

title: HTTP, GraphQL, and RUES APIs
summary: Use canonical GraphQL for chain and archive queries, RUES-style /on routes for contracts, transactions, drivers, events, proofs, node operations, and low-level tooling.
beginnerUse: Choose this only after SDK access works or when you are building infrastructure, indexers, dashboards, or diagnostic tools.
safeNextStep: Prefer W3sper for app code; use raw API calls for specific low-level checks.

title: Moonlight public and Phoenix shielded accounts
summary: Build with Dusk account models deliberately: Moonlight for public transfers and exchange-style flows, Phoenix for shielded addresses and privacy-preserving transfer models.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you need to understand which address type your app, exchange, or wallet flow expects.
safeNextStep: Use Moonlight for public deposit/withdrawal flows; do not mix Phoenix shielded addresses into Moonlight-only integrations.

title: Citadel 2 private identity and access
summary: Build private credential and access flows where a user proves a valid license exists without publishing personal attributes or the exact license used on-chain.
beginnerUse: Choose this after you understand the native contract and wallet flow; Citadel is a specialized identity protocol, not the first DuskDS tutorial.
safeNextStep: Read the protocol page and threat model before planning any production identity or compliance workflow.

title: Rusk nodes, localnet, archive, and prover modes
summary: Run or integrate with Rusk for HTTP/GraphQL APIs, local development nodes, archive history, and prover services for Phoenix transaction flows.
beginnerUse: Choose this when public endpoints are not enough, you need archive history, or you are building infra rather than a simple dApp.
safeNextStep: Start with public Testnet endpoints for app work; use Rusk local dev or archive mode when you need controlled infrastructure.

title: Native standards, DRC20, and contract guards
summary: Use Dusk-native contract standards for access control, Ownable/Ownable2Step, pausing, nonce and replay guards, threshold multisig, reentrancy protection, DRC20 primitives, and reference contracts.
beginnerUse: Choose this after your first Forge contract works and you need safer building blocks for tokens, admin controls, or governed contract actions.
safeNextStep: Study the standards crate and reference examples, then keep the first production use behind tests, audits, and verifiable builds.

title: Stake Abstraction and programmable staking
summary: Build contracts that own or manage stake and rewards for staking pools, liquid staking, delegated staking services, reward splits, or other programmable staking logic.
beginnerUse: Choose this only after you understand native contract calls, the Transfer Contract, and the Stake Contract; it is not the normal wallet staking flow.
safeNextStep: Follow the official Stake Abstraction guide, use contract-to-contract transfer into stake_from_contract, and implement receive_unstake and receive_reward callbacks deliberately.

title: Exchange deposits, withdrawals, and history
summary: Support Moonlight public deposits and withdrawals, archive-backed history queries, finality checks, memo-based user attribution, and cold-storage patterns.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you are building exchange, custodial, accounting, or backend payment infrastructure.
safeNextStep: Use W3sper for transaction construction and archive GraphQL for historical Moonlight transfer history.

title: Deterministic and verifiable builds
summary: Build smart contracts in locked Docker environments so developers and auditors can reproduce the same WASM artifact from the same source.
beginnerUse: Choose this after local Forge builds pass and before treating a contract artifact as production-ready or audit-ready.
safeNextStep: Use the verified local build first, then add Dockerized verifiable builds as a release gate.

title: Token, e-money, and multisig contract examples
summary: Study transparent token, electronic money token, and BLS multisig contracts for native Dusk patterns such as balances, allowances, events, nonces, deposits, and multi-key control.
beginnerUse: Choose this when the minimal starter is too small and you need a more realistic contract model to learn from.
safeNextStep: Treat transparent-token and electronic-money-token as unstable examples; do not copy them into production without review.

title: Dusk web app utilities with Duskit
summary: Use Dusk-maintained UI components, CSS, icons, HTTP helpers, encoding utilities, stores, actions, and test helpers for Dusk web applications.
beginnerUse: Choose this when you are moving from scripts into a reusable web app and want Dusk-native UI and helper packages.
safeNextStep: Keep wallet and network logic separate: Dusk Connect for wallet discovery, W3sper or node APIs for network behavior, Duskit for app utilities.

title: Protocol contributions, DIPs, grants, and audits
summary: Use Dusk Improvement Proposals for protocol-level changes, grants for ecosystem projects, and audit reports as production-risk context.
beginnerUse: Choose this when your project needs a protocol change, ecosystem funding, or a security-history review before launch.
safeNextStep: Review existing DIPs before proposing changes and read audit context before making security claims.

title: Regulated Solidity asset patterns
summary: Dusk-network forks of T-REX and OnchainID expose ERC-3643 and on-chain identity contract patterns that may inform regulated DuskEVM applications, but no Dusk-specific guided deployment path was found.
beginnerUse: Treat this as advanced source context if your Solidity project involves identity registries, claim issuers, compliance checks, or regulated token transfers.
safeNextStep: Build the normal DuskEVM loop first, then review T-REX and OnchainID as external Solidity patterns before deciding whether they fit your project.
